name: swift-concurrency
description: Expert guidance on Swift Concurrency best practices, patterns, and implementation. Use when developers mention: (1) Swift Concurrency, async/await, actors, or tasks, (2) "use Swift Concurrency" or "modern concurrency patterns", (3) migrating to Swift 6, (4) data races or thread safety issues, (5) refactoring closures to async/await, (6) @MainActor, Sendable, or actor isolation, (7) concurrent code architecture or performance optimization.
capabilities: Provides async/await patterns and best practices for network requests and parallel operations
Guides on actor isolation, @MainActor usage, and preventing data races
Explains Sendable conformance for thread-safe type sharing across isolation domains
Covers Task creation, cancellation, task groups, and structured vs unstructured tasks
Details Swift 6 migration strategies including strict concurrency checking
Addresses Core Data integration, testing concurrent code, and performance optimization
author: AvdLee
securitySummary: Pure documentation skill containing only markdown reference files. No executable code, no network calls, no file system access, no environment access, and no external commands. Provides safe educational guidance on Swift Concurrency patterns.
valueStatement: Swift Concurrency introduces async/await, actors, and structured concurrency. This skill provides expert guidance on adopting these patterns safely, avoiding data races, and migrating existing codebases to Swift 6 with compile-time thread safety.
limitations: Does not write or modify user code directly
Cannot execute Swift code or run tests
Does not provide project setup or Xcode configuration
Does not integrate with external build tools or CI/CD systems

useCases.title: Adopt async/await patterns
useCases.description: Migrate callback-based networking code to modern async/await syntax with proper error handling

useCases.title: Implement thread safety
useCases.description: Use actors and Sendable types to protect shared mutable state and prevent data races

useCases.title: Migrate to Swift 6
useCases.description: Navigate strict concurrency checking, enable gradual adoption, and fix isolation warnings

promptTemplates.title: Basic async conversion
promptTemplates.prompt: Convert this completion handler based network call to use async/await syntax: [paste closure-based code]

promptTemplates.title: Actor implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: Refactor this class with shared mutable state to use an actor for thread-safe access: [paste class code]

promptTemplates.title: Sendable conformance
promptTemplates.prompt: Make this struct or class Sendable compliant and explain why it is or is not safe to transfer across isolation boundaries: [paste type definition]

promptTemplates.title: Swift 6 migration
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me migrate this Swift 5 code to Swift 6. Fix these concurrency warnings: [paste code with diagnostics]

bestPractices: Prefer structured concurrency with task groups over detached tasks for automatic cancellation
Use actors for mutable shared state instead of locks or dispatch queues
Make types Sendable when passing across isolation boundaries to enable compile-time safety
Complete all actor state modifications before suspension points to prevent reentrancy bugs
antiPatterns: Using @MainActor as a blanket fix without justifying main thread necessity
Using @unchecked Sendable to silence warnings without proving thread safety
Keeping long-running work on actors that causes unnecessary contention
Assuming state is unchanged after await due to actor reentrancy

faq.question: Which Swift versions support concurrency features?
faq.answer: Async/await requires Swift 5.5+. Actors, task groups, and AsyncSequence shipped in Swift 5.5. Swift 6 enables complete strict concurrency checking by default.

faq.question: What are the main concurrency limits?
faq.answer: Tasks cannot exceed available thread pool. Actors serialize access which can cause contention. Task creation has overhead so avoid spawning thousands of tasks for trivial work.

faq.question: How does this integrate with existing code?
faq.answer: Use Task {} to bridge sync to async contexts. Wrap completion handlers with withCheckedContinuation. Gradually adopt concurrency module by module.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using this skill?
faq.answer: This skill only provides guidance documentation. No code execution, no data access, no network calls. All advice is read-only guidance.

faq.question: Why do I see Sendable warnings?
faq.answer: Swift enforces compile-time thread safety. Types crossing isolation boundaries must be Sendable. Use value types, actors, or @unchecked Sendable only with documented justification.

faq.question: How does this compare to Combine?
faq.answer: Swift Concurrency provides structured, imperative async code. Combine uses reactive streams. For simple async operations, async/await is simpler. Combine excels at complex event transformations.
